orbit_connections.name: Grover Cleveland (X00770) — his pupil at Clinton Academy
orbit_connections.relationship: Anthony taught Cleveland at Clinton Academy, Clinton NY, circa 1851
orbit_connections.notes: Cleveland was later the 22nd and 24th President of the United States

notable.notable_blurb: Illinois lawyer and judge active in the founding

burial.plot_notes: Graceland Cemetery, Chicago IL — family plot of 12 burials he purchased

education.notes: Read law afterward with Timothy Dwight V per Wikipedia (chronological issue: Tim V was 22 + theology tutor; more plausibly with Theodore William Dwight HD1989, his Hamilton Law professor 1846-58)

career.notes: Admitted NY bar May 1851; one year Sterling IL; returned East July 1852 to marry Mary Dwight; settled Chicago November 1852. Published a digest of the Illinois Reports within two years (with wife's aid). Elected Chicago City Attorney then Corporation Counsel.

Until consolidation with Chicago and North Western Railway 1864. Then employed by non-consenting stockholders+bondholders in litigation to break up consolidation. Argued before Justice David Davis (US Supreme Court) and Samuel Hubbel Treat Jr. (Springfield); won for his clients. Published book on railway consolidation 1865.
Under Mayor Monroe Heath; part of the great reform movement with Thomas Hoyne.
Elected November 1880 by a large majority. Identified with many of Chicago's public improvements — graded streets, waterworks, public parks, public library, cemeteries.

narrative_blocks.header: Read law in Dwight family, married in
narrative_blocks.body: After graduating Hamilton with high honors in 1850 he read law in the Dwight family orbit (Theodore William Dwight was his Hamilton law professor). In May 1851 he was admitted to the New York bar; in July 1852 he married Mary Dwight — granddaughter of Timothy Dwight IV and sister of the future 12th Yale president Timothy Dwight V.

narrative_blocks.header: Republican Party founder for over a quarter century
narrative_blocks.body: Raised a Quaker, he took part in the formation of the Republican Party and led the organization in Illinois for more than 25 years. He was a delegate to the 1880 Republican National Convention and aided in the nomination of James A. Garfield for President.

As General Solicitor of the Galena and Chicago Union Railroad 1858-1864 he argued the major case challenging the railroad's consolidation with the Chicago and North Western. The case went before Justice David Davis of the U.S. Supreme Court and Samuel Hubbel Treat Jr. of Springfield IL; Anthony won for his non-consenting stockholder clients and published a book on railway consolidation in 1865.

narrative_blocks.header: Married two Dwight sisters, 13 years apart
narrative_blocks.body: On 14 July 1852 he married Mary Dwight, 27, in Catskill NY — they had four children and lived in Chicago. Mary died at 39 in February 1864. On 11 January 1865 he married her younger sister Elizabeth Dwight, 33 — she bore no children and devoted her life to raising Mary's four before her own death at 38 in 1870.

narrative_blocks.header: He taught at Clinton Academy before the law
narrative_blocks.body: Before he turned to law, Anthony taught at Clinton Academy in upstate New York. One of his pupils was a boy named Grover Cleveland, the future president.

In July 1852 she married Elliott Anthony — a prominent Chicago lawyer who would later sit on the Illinois Circuit Court. They moved to Chicago where she bore four children. She died at 39 in Chicago in February 1864 and was buried at Graceland Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband later married her younger sister Elizabeth
narrative_blocks.body: After Mary's 1864 death her widower Elliott Anthony married her younger sister Elizabeth Dwight in January 1865 as his second wife; Elizabeth bore no children and devoted her life to raising Mary's four. The two Dwight sisters thus shared one Chicago lawyer husband across two marriages.

Through her son Charles Elliott Anthony who married Mary P. Green in Chicago 1879, she became grandmother of Mary Elizabeth 'Bessie' Anthony Horne — the 4-time consecutive Women's Western Golf Association champion 1901-1904. Bessie's 1903 marriage to Pittsburgh Pictorialist photographer Bernard Shea Horne Sr extended the Dwight-Anthony line into Pittsburgh's industrial dynasty.

narrative_blocks.header: Physician who became treasurer of Hamilton College
narrative_blocks.body: A physician in Massachusetts and New York, in 1831 he relocated to Clinton NY to become treasurer of Hamilton College — where his brother Sereno Edwards Dwight would serve as the college's third president two years later. His daughter-line married into the Hamilton academic community.

narrative_blocks.header: Father of Columbia Law School's founding dean
narrative_blocks.body: Two of his sons became major American scholars: Benjamin Woodbridge Dwight, an educator and author, and Theodore William Dwight, who founded the Columbia Law School and served as its first dean for over thirty years. The Dwight line of academic prominence ran through his household.

narrative_blocks.header: Married as an invalid, expecting to die
narrative_blocks.body: Weakened by a severe cold made worse by 'too unremitted attention to some favorite tasks in embroidery she had imposed upon herself,' she could not sit up and coughed blood with any exertion. In spring 1815 she was married to physician Benjamin Woolsey Dwight at her uncle's house in South Hadley MA in her sick gown, expecting to live only a little while.

narrative_blocks.header: Recovered through her physician husband's care
narrative_blocks.body: Her new husband, a trained physician, applied vigorous medical means over several months and she recovered her strength enough to enjoy comfortable health for many years afterward. She lived another forty-six years and bore five children including the future founder of Columbia Law School Theodore William Dwight and the educator-genealogist Benjamin Woodbridge Dwight.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of Yale, Hamilton, and Columbia educators
narrative_blocks.body: Her six children spanned the New England intellectual elite: Benjamin Woodbridge Dwight (Yale Theology + genealogist), Theodore William Dwight (Columbia Law founder), Sophia (cousin marriage), Mary (Chicago Judge Anthony's first wife), Edward Woolsey (Wisconsin Greenback Party), and Elizabeth (Mary's widower's second wife).

Three of her daughters' generation in the Dwight family practiced sister-replacement marriage as a stabilization strategy after tuberculosis-era widow-hood: Julia Dwight then Mary Woolsey Dwight to Dr. Lyman (1847+1855); her own daughter Mary then Elizabeth to Judge Anthony (1852+1865); cousin Lucy Waterman then Elizabeth Adams to Rev. Edward Strong Dwight (1849+1864).
